title: May Sky Show: Shooting Stars, a Moon-Venus Meetup, and a Rare Blue Moon!
summary: Grab a blanket and look up — May's night sky is putting on a show! First, the Eta Aquarid meteor shower peaks around May 5th and 6th. These shooting stars are pieces of Halley's Comet, burning up as they hit Earth's air. They zip across the sky at 40 miles per second — so fast they can leave glowing trails. Under a dark sky, you might see up to 50 meteors an hour. The best time? In the hours before dawn, facing east. Just get away from bright lights, put your phone down, and let your eyes adjust for about 20 minutes. On May 18th, look west right after sunset. The thin crescent Moon will be right next to Venus, the super-bright 'Evening Star.' They'll look close together, but they're actually millions of miles apart. It's an easy, beautiful sight — no telescope needed. Finally, May 31st brings a Full Moon. But this one is special: it's a Blue Moon! That doesn't mean it turns blue. A Blue Moon is the second Full Moon in a single month, which only happens once in a while — that's where the saying 'once in a blue moon' comes from. So set your alarms, mark your calendars, and enjoy the cosmic wonders this May!
why_it_matters: The night sky is like a free outdoor movie that changes every night — looking up can fill you with wonder and remind you that we live on a planet moving through a huge, amazing universe.

keywords.explanation: A yearly display of shooting stars that comes from the dusty trail of Halley’s Comet.
A famous comet that swings past Earth every 76 years, leaving behind dust that becomes shooting stars.
Pieces of space rock or dust that burn up in Earth's air and look like bright streaks across the sky.
The thin, curved shape of the Moon when only a sliver of it is lit up.
The second planet from the Sun, often called the 'Evening Star' because it shines so brightly in the west.
The second full Moon in a single month — a rare event that gives us the saying 'once in a blue moon'.

questions.question: What is the Eta Aquarid meteor shower made of?
When is the best time to watch the meteor shower?
What will the Moon be next to on May 18th?
Why is the May 31st Full Moon called a Blue Moon?
options: It is the second Full Moon in a single month
It is the biggest Moon of the year
It is made of blue cheese
questions.correct_answer: It is the second Full Moon in a single month

questions.question: How fast do Eta Aquarid meteors zip across the sky?
What should you do to see the meteor shower well?
options: Look toward the west right after sunset
Get away from bright lights and let your eyes adjust
Stare at your phone screen to locate the meteors
questions.correct_answer: Get away from bright lights and let your eyes adjust

background_read: Halley’s Comet is one of the most famous comets. It last visited our skies in 1986 and will return in 2061. Each time it swings close to the Sun, it leaves a trail of dust that Earth passes through, creating meteor showers like the Eta Aquarids.

Venus is often so bright that it looks like a shining lamp in the evening. Despite the Moon appearing close to it on May 18th, Venus is actually millions of miles farther away — the two just line up from our viewpoint on Earth.

A Blue Moon isn’t blue in color. It’s a calendar trick: because the Moon’s cycle is about 29.5 days, occasionally a month gets two full Moons. The second one is called a Blue Moon, and the phrase reminds us it doesn’t happen very often.
Article_Structure: WHO: Stargazers and anyone who loves looking at the night sky.
WHAT: Three sky events — the Eta Aquarid meteor shower, a close meeting of the Moon and Venus, and a rare Blue Moon.
WHERE: All can be seen from Earth; meteors in the east before dawn, Moon and Venus in the west after sunset, and the Blue Moon high in the sky on May 31st.
WHY: These events happen because Earth passes through comet dust, the Moon and Venus line up in our view, and the calendar gives us a second full Moon in one month.

perspectives.description: These are perfect chances to see natural wonders without expensive gear. Just a dark spot and a little patience can give you a front‑row seat.
Bright city lights often hide faint meteors. Finding a darker place takes extra effort, but it can make the show much more magical.
Meteor showers remind us that Earth is always sweeping up ancient dust from space. Studying them helps us understand how our solar system works and how comets change over time.
